title: Arrays & 2D Arrays
description: Master Java array declaration, initialization, traversal, and 2D grid manipulation — the foundation of nearly every CSE110 exam code trace and output prediction question.

sections.content: Arrays are the most exam-tested data structure in CSE110. Expect questions on **array initialization syntax**, **index-out-of-bounds** tracing, **2D array traversal order**, **array as method parameters**, and **output prediction** involving nested loops over grids. A single misread of row-vs-column order costs you an entire multi-part question.

In Java, an array is an **object** allocated on the heap. Declaration creates a reference variable; `new` allocates the actual memory. Arrays have a fixed **length** field and are **zero-indexed**. Default values are assigned automatically: `0` for int, `0.0` for double, `false` for boolean, `null` for objects.

rows: Two-step; arr holds null until new
Size inferred from literal; no `new` needed
Useful when passing array literal to method

sections.title: 1D Array Fundamentals

sections.label: 🚨 .length vs .length() — The #1 Array Mistake
sections.content: Arrays use `.length` (a **field**, no parentheses). Strings use `.length()` (a **method**, with parentheses). Writing `arr.length()` on an array is a **compile-time error**. Conversely, writing `str.length` on a String is also a compile-time error. This distinction appears on almost every CSE110 exam.

Accessing an index that doesn't exist — either negative or ≥ array.length — throws `ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException` at **runtime**. This is one of the most common runtime errors tested in error-identification questions.

rows: `arr[5]` → out of bounds; loop `i <= 5` → OOB on last iteration
`arr[n]` → out of bounds; loop `i <= arr.length` → OOB
Any access is out of bounds; even `arr[0]`

sections.content: A 2D array in Java is an **array of arrays**. `int[][] grid = new int[rows][cols]` creates `rows` row-arrays each of length `cols`. Access uses `grid[row][col]`. The outer loop iterates over **rows** (first index), the inner loop over **columns** (second index). Getting this order wrong is the single most common 2D array exam mistake.

sections.title: 2D Array Declaration, Initialization, and Traversal

sections.label: Jagged Arrays — Not All Rows Equal
sections.content: In Java, `int[][] arr = new int[3][]` creates 3 rows with **no columns yet**. You can then do `arr[0] = new int[2]; arr[1] = new int[4]; arr[2] = new int[1]` — each row has a different length. This is a **jagged array**. `arr.length` = 3 (rows), but `arr[0].length` = 2, `arr[1].length` = 4. Always use `grid[row].length` for column count, never assume it equals `grid[0].length`.

sections.title: Four Key 2D Traversal Patterns

sections.content: Arrays in Java are **objects**, so when passed to a method, the method receives a **copy of the reference** — not a copy of the data. This means a method **can modify** the original array's elements. However, reassigning the parameter (`arr = new int[5]`) inside the method does **not** affect the caller's reference — only the local copy of the reference changes.

sections.title: Array Reference Passing Behavior

sections.label: Returning vs Modifying Arrays
sections.content: Prefer **returning a new array** from methods when the transformation produces logically different data. **Modify in-place** only when the method's documented purpose is mutation (e.g., `sort`, `reverse`). This avoids subtle bugs where the caller doesn't realize their array was changed.

rows: Sorts array in-place, ascending, O(n log n)
Returns new array of given length (truncates or pads with 0)
True if same length and same elements (use this, not ==)
Returns "[1, 2, 3]" style string — great for debugging

sections.title: Arrays Utility Showcase
Exercise 1: 2D Array Loop Trace
sections.explanation: The condition `(i+j) % 2 == 0` selects cells where row+col is even. Trace each cell: (0,0)=1✓, (0,1)=2✗, (0,2)=3✓, (1,0)=4✗, (1,1)=5✓, (1,2)=6✗, (2,0)=7✓, (2,1)=8✗, (2,2)=9✓. These are the checkerboard positions with even sum: values 1, 3, 5, 7, 9 — coincidentally, the odd values in this matrix and the main+anti-diagonal pattern. Printed with a trailing space after each.

sections.title: Exercise 2: Array Reference and Mutation Trace
sections.explanation: Line by line: `x = {10, 20, 30}`. `modify(x, 1, 99)` passes the array reference — `arr[1] = 99` modifies the original. So `x[1]` is now 99 → prints `99`. `reset(x)` assigns `arr = new int[]{0,0,0}` which only changes the local copy of the reference inside `reset` — `x` in main still points to the original array `{10, 99, 30}`. So `x[0]+" "+x[1]+" "+x[2]` = `10 99 30`.

sections.title: Exercise 3: Off-by-One and Dimension Error
sections.error: Line 4: `i <= grid.length` should be `i < grid.length`. With `<=`, i reaches 3 and `grid[3]` throws ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException (runtime error). Line 5: `j <= grid.length` uses row count (3) instead of column count — should be `j < grid[i].length` (which is 4). With `<=` and grid.length=3, j only reaches 3, missing column 3 entirely — logical error. Both are runtime/logical errors.
sections.explanation: Two bugs: (1) `i <= grid.length` uses `<=` which allows i=3 — accessing grid[3] on a 3-row array (indices 0–2) throws ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException at runtime. Fix: `i < grid.length`. (2) `j <= grid.length` uses the row count (3) for the column limit — the grid has 4 columns (indices 0–3) so this misses column 3 entirely. Fix: `j < grid[i].length`. The corrected grid[2][3] = 2+3 = 5.

sections.title: Exercise 4: Column-Sum Pattern
sections.explanation: The outer loop iterates over columns (0, 1, 2). For each column, the inner loop sums all rows. Column 0: mat[0][0]+mat[1][0]+mat[2][0] = 1+2+3 = 6. Column 1: mat[0][1]+mat[1][1]+mat[2][1] = 4+5+6 = 15. Column 2: mat[0][2]+mat[1][2]+mat[2][2] = 7+8+9 = 24. Note the outer loop goes over *columns* (using mat[0].length) and the inner over *rows* (using mat.length) — a column-major traversal.

sections.title: Exercise 5: Transpose a Square Matrix
sections.approach: Only swap elements above the main diagonal (where j > i) to avoid double-swapping. Loop i from 0 to n-1, j from i+1 to n-1, and swap mat[i][j] with mat[j][i]. Then print the matrix row by row.
sections.explanation: The key insight: only iterate where `j > i` (upper triangle). If you also loop where j < i, you swap each pair twice, ending up with the original. The three-way swap using `temp` is necessary — without it, writing `mat[i][j] = mat[j][i]` overwrites the value before it's saved. The diagonal (i==j) never needs swapping. Time: O(n²). Space: O(1) in-place. The method modifies the original array since arrays are passed by reference in Java.

items: **Array length**: use `.length` (field, no parentheses) — using `.length()` is a compile-time error
**Index bounds**: valid indices are 0 to `arr.length - 1`; accessing `arr[arr.length]` throws `ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException`
**2D dimensions**: `grid.length` = row count; `grid[i].length` = column count of row i — never use `grid.length` for column count
**Array parameter passing**: arrays passed by reference — methods **can** modify element values; reassigning the parameter (`arr = new int[]{}`) does **not** affect caller
**Equality check**: use `Arrays.equals(a, b)` for content equality; `a == b` only checks if they are the same object
**Traversal order**: for 2D, outer loop = rows (`i`), inner loop = columns (`j`); swapping them gives column-major order
**Jagged arrays**: Java allows rows of different lengths; always use `grid[i].length` not a hardcoded column count
**Enhanced for-each**: `for (int x : arr)` iterates values read-only; you cannot modify array elements through the loop variable
